sections.plain_summary: IBD in remission means your inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis) is currently well-controlled with little to no active inflammation. You may have few or no symptoms, but the condition remains present and can flare up again. The goal is to maintain remission through medication adherence, lifestyle choices, and regular monitoring.
sections.detailed_summary: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) encompasses two main conditions: Crohn's disease, which can affect any part of the digestive tract, and ulcerative colitis, which affects the colon and rectum. Remission means the inflammation is suppressed—either through medication, dietary changes, or both—and symptoms are absent or minimal. Remission is not a cure; the underlying condition persists, and flares can occur if maintenance therapy is stopped or triggers are encountered. People in remission often experience normal bowel function, reduced pain, and improved quality of life, but must remain vigilant about adherence to treatment and early warning signs of relapse.

prevalence.summary: About 1 in 100 to 1 in 200 people in developed countries have IBD, with rates rising globally.
prevalence.estimate: 0.5–1.5% of the population in North America and Europe; higher in Scandinavian countries
prevalence.notes: Prevalence has been increasing over the past 20 years, particularly in younger people and in newly industrialized countries. Rates vary significantly by geography and ethnicity. Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis occur in roughly equal proportions.

subgroups.name: Crohn's disease in remission
subgroups.description: Inflammation of any part of the digestive tract (mouth to anus) is controlled. Remission may involve patchy areas of inflammation with normal tissue in between.
distinguishing_features: Can affect any layer of the bowel wall, not just the lining
May present with complications like fistulas, strictures, or abscesses even in remission
Nutritional deficiencies are more common due to reduced absorption
May require more aggressive maintenance therapy to prevent complications

subgroups.name: Ulcerative colitis in remission
subgroups.description: Inflammation of the colon and rectum is suppressed. Remission involves healing of the mucosal lining.
distinguishing_features: Inflammation is limited to the innermost lining (mucosa) of the colon
Continuous inflammation pattern (not patchy) when active
Lower risk of fistulas and strictures compared to Crohn's
May be cured by colectomy (surgical removal of colon), whereas Crohn's can recur after surgery

subgroups.name: Early remission (0–3 months)
subgroups.description: Recently achieved remission, still establishing stable maintenance therapy and risk of early relapse.
distinguishing_features: Higher risk of flare within first few months if medication is adjusted
May still experience mild residual symptoms or healing-related changes
Requires closer monitoring and frequent clinic visits

subgroups.name: Sustained remission (>1 year)
subgroups.description: Long-term remission with stable disease control on maintenance therapy.
distinguishing_features: Lower flare risk if medication is continued
Quality of life typically approaches that of non-IBD individuals
May allow reduction in monitoring frequency if clinically stable

symptoms.symptom: Absence of diarrhea or return to normal bowel pattern
symptoms.notes: In remission, bowel habits should normalize. Occasional loose stools are normal but frequent diarrhea may signal early flare.

symptoms.symptom: Absence of abdominal pain or cramping
symptoms.notes: Complete or near-complete resolution of pain is expected in remission. Mild discomfort with certain foods is common but severe pain suggests relapse.

symptoms.symptom: No blood or mucus in stool
symptoms.notes: A key marker of remission. Any recurrence of blood in stool warrants medical evaluation.

symptoms.symptom: Normal energy levels and reduced fatigue
symptoms.notes: Fatigue often persists due to residual anemia or medication side effects even in remission; should improve gradually.
Appetite typically returns in remission as inflammation decreases and symptoms resolve.

symptoms.symptom: Reduced need for frequent bathroom visits
symptoms.notes: Urinary frequency may decrease to pre-flare levels, though some people retain slightly increased urgency.
Joint pain, eye inflammation, or skin lesions (erythema nodosum, pyoderma gangrenosum) may persist or flare independently of bowel disease.

symptoms.symptom: Minimal or no rectal urgency at night
symptoms.notes: Night-time bowel symptoms typically resolve in remission; persistence may indicate inadequate control.

key_patterns: Absence of blood, mucus, or diarrhea for weeks at a time—consistent normal or near-normal bowel pattern
Pain-free or near pain-free days without acute discomfort after meals
Stable weight or gradual weight gain, indicating improved nutrition absorption
Energy levels sufficient for work and daily activities without afternoon crashes
Sleep uninterrupted by nighttime bowel symptoms
Ability to eat a wider variety of foods without immediate symptom flare
Mental clarity and mood stability (reduced stress and anxiety related to disease activity)

at_home.name: Symptom and bowel diary
at_home.description: Record date, time, bowel movement frequency, consistency (Bristol scale), presence of blood/mucus, abdominal pain (0–10 scale), and foods eaten. Maintain for 2–4 weeks to establish baseline and identify patterns. Share with your doctor at appointments.
at_home.safety_notes: Diary is passive observation only. Do not delay medical care if you experience severe pain, high fever, or heavy bleeding.

at_home.name: Fecal calprotectin home test kit
at_home.description: Some home kits allow collection of a small stool sample to mail to a lab for calprotectin measurement (marker of intestinal inflammation). Provides objective data to discuss with your doctor. Follow kit instructions for collection and shipping.
at_home.safety_notes: Home kits are supplementary; they do not replace clinical assessment. Discuss results with your gastroenterologist. Elevated levels warrant medical review even if you feel well.

at_home.name: Weight and waist circumference tracking
at_home.description: Weigh yourself weekly at the same time of day and measure waist circumference monthly. Stable or gradual increase suggests good nutritional status and disease control.
at_home.safety_notes: Rapid weight loss (>2 lbs per week) or gain may signal disease flare or other problem; contact your doctor.

clinical.name: Fecal calprotectin or lactoferrin
clinical.description: Stool test measuring proteins released by inflamed intestinal lining. Levels <50–100 μg/g generally indicate remission; higher levels suggest active inflammation even if symptoms are mild. Non-invasive and sensitive marker of mucosal healing.
clinical.availability: Ordered by gastroenterologist or primary care doctor; results available in 1–2 weeks. Widely available in developed countries.

clinical.name: C-reactive protein (CRP) and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR)
clinical.description: Blood tests measuring systemic inflammation. CRP <5 mg/L and ESR <20 mm/hr generally support remission. Less specific than fecal calprotectin but useful in combination. May remain elevated in some people in remission.
clinical.availability: Routine blood work ordered by any physician; results available in 1–2 days.

clinical.name: Complete blood count (CBC) and comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP)
clinical.description: Routine blood work assessing hemoglobin (for anemia), white blood cell count, albumin, and kidney and liver function. Normalization of hemoglobin and albumin support remission and good nutritional status.
clinical.availability: Routine labs ordered by any physician; results available in 1–2 days.

clinical.name: Colonoscopy with ileoscopy (Crohn's) or sigmoidoscopy (ulcerative colitis)
clinical.description: Direct visual inspection of the intestinal lining with biopsy sampling. Gold standard for assessing mucosal healing and confirming remission. Can detect microscopic inflammation not visible to the naked eye. Usually performed annually or at specific intervals per treatment protocol.
clinical.availability: Ordered by gastroenterologist; procedure performed at hospital or endoscopy center under sedation. Requires bowel preparation.

clinical.name: Fecal immunochemical test (FIT) for occult blood
clinical.description: Stool test detecting microscopic blood not visible to the eye. Negative FIT in the setting of normal symptoms supports remission.
clinical.availability: Ordered by any physician; stool sample collected at home and mailed to lab. Results available in 1–2 weeks.

clinical.name: Magnetic resonance enterography (MRE) or CT enterography
clinical.description: Imaging studies visualizing the entire small bowel and wall thickness. Useful for assessing Crohn's disease burden, detecting fistulas or strictures, and confirming mucosal healing. Less frequently used in remission maintenance but valuable for complication assessment.
clinical.availability: Ordered by gastroenterologist; performed at imaging center. Requires bowel preparation and contrast. May use radiation (CT) or not (MRI).

clinical.name: Ultrasound of the bowel
clinical.description: Point-of-care ultrasound assessing bowel wall thickness and blood flow (Doppler). Emerging as a non-invasive, radiation-free tool to assess disease activity. Less widely available and operator-dependent.
clinical.availability: Specialized gastroenterology centers with expertise; not routine at all facilities.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: IBS and IBD are distinct but can coexist. IBS-like symptoms (bloating, altered bowel habits) may persist in IBD remission due to altered gut motility or visceral hypersensitivity, making differentiation difficult. True IBD involves inflammation; IBS does not.

related_conditions.name: Microscopic colitis
related_conditions.explanation: Causes chronic diarrhea but is not IBD and does not respond to IBD therapies in the same way. Can coexist with IBD. Diagnosis requires colonoscopy with biopsy.

related_conditions.name: Celiac disease
related_conditions.explanation: More common in people with IBD. Gluten sensitivity can worsen IBD symptoms or cause overlapping symptoms. Screening (tissue transglutaminase antibody) recommended at IBD diagnosis.

related_conditions.name: Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC)
related_conditions.explanation: Autoimmune liver condition affecting 1–4% of people with ulcerative colitis. Occurs independently of bowel disease activity. Requires monitoring with liver enzymes and imaging.

related_conditions.name: Peripheral arthropathy and axial spondylitis
related_conditions.explanation: Joint inflammation associated with IBD, especially in those with HLA-B27 gene. Can flare independent of bowel disease activity. Managed with NSAIDs (with caution in IBD) or biologics.

related_conditions.name: Anemia
related_conditions.explanation: Iron-deficiency anemia common in Crohn's disease due to chronic blood loss and malabsorption. B12 and folate deficiency possible with terminal ileum involvement. Resolves with disease control and iron/B12 supplementation.

related_conditions.name: Dermatologic manifestations (erythema nodosum, pyoderma gangrenosum)
related_conditions.explanation: Skin conditions associated with IBD, typically flaring with bowel activity but sometimes independent. Erythema nodosum is more common; pyoderma gangrenosum is rarer and more severe.

related_conditions.name: Nutritional deficiencies (vitamin D, B12, iron, protein)
related_conditions.explanation: Malabsorption, reduced intake during flares, and medication effects (methotrexate reduces folate) can cause deficiencies. These may persist in remission if not actively replaced.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety and depression
related_conditions.explanation: Bidirectional relationship: chronic disease increases risk of mental health conditions, and psychological stress can trigger flares. Common in IBD even during remission.

related_conditions.name: Increased cardiovascular and thrombotic risk
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic inflammation in IBD increases risk of blood clots (venous thromboembolism) and cardiovascular events. Risk decreases but does not normalize in remission.

strategies.name: Medication adherence (maintenance therapy)
strategies.description: Continue prescribed medications (aminosalicylates, corticosteroids, immunosuppressants, or biologics) exactly as directed even if feeling well. Remission is maintained by these drugs; stopping or skipping doses significantly increases relapse risk. Set phone reminders or use pill organizers to ensure consistency.
strategies.notes: Temptation to stop medication during remission is common but risky. Discuss any desire to reduce or stop medications with your gastroenterologist; some people may be candidates for dose reduction after sustained remission, but this requires careful monitoring.

strategies.name: Identify and avoid personal trigger foods
strategies.description: Keep a food diary for 2–4 weeks to identify foods that consistently cause symptoms (bloating, pain, loose stool). Common triggers include high-fat foods, high-fiber (in some), dairy, alcohol, and caffeine, but triggers are highly individual. Avoid identified triggers; reintroduce cautiously every few months to see if tolerance improves.
strategies.notes: Trigger avoidance is person-specific and varies. Some foods are troublesome during flares but well-tolerated in remission. Work with a dietitian familiar with IBD for personalized guidance.

strategies.name: Anti-inflammatory diet pattern
strategies.description: Adopt a pattern emphasizing lean proteins, low-FODMAP vegetables, refined grains, and omega-3 sources (fish, flax, walnuts) while minimizing processed foods, high-fat items, and alcohol. Mediterranean-style or IBD-specific anti-inflammatory diet patterns show benefit in maintaining remission.
strategies.notes: No single diet works for all IBD patients. Low-FODMAP diet helps some but may restrict beneficial fiber in others. Work with a registered dietitian on IBD to personalize.

strategies.name: Optimize micronutrient supplementation
strategies.description: Blood work often reveals deficiencies in iron, B12, folate, vitamin D, and zinc due to malabsorption or previous losses. Supplement based on lab results: iron (if anemia present), B12 (especially post-terminal ileum involvement), vitamin D (target 30–50 ng/mL), and zinc as needed. Recheck levels every 6–12 months.
strategies.notes: Do not self-supplement without testing; some supplements (e.g., iron) can cause GI upset in IBD. Discuss dosing and form (pill vs. liquid, frequency) with your doctor or dietitian.

strategies.name: Stress reduction and psychological support
strategies.description: Chronic stress can trigger flares. Practice mindfulness, deep breathing, yoga, or cognitive behavioral therapy. Consider therapy or support groups to address anxiety or depression related to chronic illness. Even 10–15 minutes daily of relaxation can help.
strategies.notes: Stress-triggered flares are real but not all flares are stress-related. Combining stress management with medical therapy is most effective.

strategies.name: Regular physical activity
strategies.description: Exercise 150 minutes per week of moderate activity (walking, swimming, cycling) improves mood, strengthens bones (important as corticosteroids increase osteoporosis risk), and may reduce flare risk. Avoid intense exercise immediately before or after eating if it triggers symptoms.
strategies.notes: Listen to your body; scale back during mild symptoms or flares. Swimming and walking are gentler on the gut than running or high-impact sports for some.

strategies.name: Sleep hygiene
strategies.description: Aim for 7–9 hours of consistent sleep. Poor sleep worsens inflammation and flare risk. Maintain regular bedtime, avoid screens 1 hour before bed, and minimize nighttime bathroom trips by taking medications and eating dinner early.
strategies.notes: Nighttime symptoms in remission should be rare; if persisting, discuss with your doctor as it may signal early relapse or other sleep disorder.

strategies.name: Hydration and electrolyte balance
strategies.description: Drink adequate water daily (especially in warm climates or during exercise). Monitor urine color (pale = well-hydrated). In remission, normal hydration is sufficient; during flares or with loose stool, electrolyte replacement (sports drinks, broths) may be needed.
strategies.notes: Caffeine and alcohol are dehydrating and may trigger symptoms in some; limit or observe effects.

strategies.name: Smoking cessation
strategies.description: If you smoke, quitting is one of the most important steps to maintain remission, especially in Crohn's disease. Smoking increases flare risk and complications. Seek professional support (quitlines, medication, behavioral counseling).
strategies.notes: Smoking worsens Crohn's disease more than ulcerative colitis. Even secondhand smoke exposure is suboptimal.

strategies.name: Probiotic use (selective)
strategies.description: Evidence for probiotics in IBD is mixed. Some people report benefit with specific strains (e.g., VSL#3); others see no effect. If interested, choose a strain studied in IBD and monitor for 4–8 weeks. Discontinue if symptoms worsen.
strategies.notes: Probiotics are not a replacement for maintenance medication. Use only as adjunctive therapy. Quality and strain matter; not all products are equal.

strategies.name: Regular monitoring appointments and testing
strategies.description: Schedule routine follow-up visits (every 3–6 months initially, then annually if stable) to assess disease control, review labs (fecal calprotectin, CBC, CMP), and adjust therapy if needed. Colonoscopy typically every 1–2 years per your gastroenterologist's protocol.
strategies.notes: Consistent monitoring catches early relapse and allows timely treatment adjustment. Skipping appointments increases risk of undetected disease progression.

things_to_avoid: Stopping or significantly reducing maintenance medication without medical guidance—high relapse risk.
NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) unless approved by your doctor—can worsen IBD and trigger flares.
Antibiotics without necessity—may disrupt gut microbiota and trigger flares; use only when medically indicated.
High-fat, high-residue, or high-fiber foods if they are personal triggers—keep a diary to identify them.
Alcohol and caffeine if they consistently trigger symptoms; limit or avoid.
Smoking and secondhand smoke exposure—significantly increases flare risk, especially in Crohn's disease.
Chronic stress without coping mechanisms—psychological stress is a known flare trigger.
Dehydration—maintain adequate fluid intake to prevent electrolyte imbalance and constipation (or worsened diarrhea).
Excess salt and processed foods—may promote inflammation; choose whole foods when possible.
Traveling without a plan—pack medications, know your destination hospital, and avoid high-risk foods when traveling internationally.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Maintenance therapy directly suppresses the underlying inflammation; even small lapses dramatically increase relapse risk. Adherence is the strongest predictor of sustained remission.
key_levers.how_to_start: Set daily phone reminders, use a pill organizer, or link medication-taking to a daily habit (e.g., breakfast). Ask your pharmacist to simplify your regimen if you're on multiple drugs.
key_levers.timeline: Effects are ongoing; missing doses within days can allow inflammation to rebound.

key_levers.lever: Identify and avoid personal trigger foods
key_levers.why_it_matters: Food triggers are highly individual. Avoiding them reduces preventable symptom flares and GI stress, allowing remission to be sustained without medication escalation.
key_levers.how_to_start: Keep a detailed food and symptom diary for 3–4 weeks. Note meal composition, time, and any symptoms 2–4 hours later. Share with a gastroenterologist or IBD dietitian.
key_levers.timeline: Patterns often emerge within 2–4 weeks of careful tracking.

key_levers.lever: Optimize vitamin D and micronutrient levels
key_levers.why_it_matters: Deficiencies impair immune function and gut healing, increasing flare risk. Vitamin D in particular has immunomodulatory effects. Correcting deficiencies improves energy, bone health, and may reduce flare frequency.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request lab testing (vitamin D, iron, B12, folate, zinc) at your next visit. Supplement based on results; recheck every 6–12 months.
key_levers.timeline: Nutrient repletion takes 2–3 months; energy and general well-being may improve within weeks.

key_levers.lever: Establish a stress management and sleep routine
key_levers.why_it_matters: Chronic stress and poor sleep promote systemic inflammation and are independent flare triggers. Even modest improvements in sleep and stress reduce flare risk and improve quality of life.
key_levers.how_to_start: Choose one technique: a 10-minute daily meditation app (Headspace, Calm), 3x weekly gentle yoga, or cognitive behavioral therapy. Set a consistent bedtime 30 minutes earlier than usual.
key_levers.timeline: Subjective improvements in mood and energy within 2–4 weeks; flare reduction may take 2–3 months to become apparent.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Smoking is the single strongest modifiable risk factor for flare and disease progression in Crohn's disease, and increases complications. Quitting dramatically improves long-term remission and outcomes.
key_levers.how_to_start: Consult your doctor about medication options (varenicline, bupropion, nicotine replacement). Enroll in a quitline (1-800-QUIT-NOW in US) for free support.
key_levers.timeline: Flare risk decreases within months of quitting; major benefit within 1–2 years.

key_levers.lever: Maintain regular medical follow-up and monitoring
key_levers.why_it_matters: Early detection of subclinical disease activity (via fecal calprotectin or endoscopy) allows preemptive therapy adjustment before clinical flare occurs. Consistent contact with your gastroenterologist ensures therapy is optimized.
key_levers.how_to_start: Schedule appointments every 3–6 months; order fecal calprotectin or CRP annually or per your doctor's protocol. Set calendar reminders.
key_levers.timeline: Ongoing; benefits manifest over months and years as disease progression is prevented.

common_mistakes: Stopping or reducing medication during remission because you 'feel better'—remission exists because of the medication, not despite it. Stopping causes relapse in 60–80% of people within a year.
Trying to 'cure' IBD through diet alone without continuing medical therapy—no diet replaces maintenance medication. Diet supports, not replaces, medical management.
Ignoring early warning signs (increase in stool frequency, blood in stool, increased fatigue) and delaying doctor contact—early intervention can prevent full flare and hospitalization.
Not tracking your disease or symptoms, making it hard to identify triggers or recognize early relapse—keep a symptom diary and share it with your doctor.
Isolating due to anxiety about the condition—social support and therapy improve outcomes. Connect with patient communities and mental health support.
Assuming all advice online applies to you—IBD is highly individual. What helps one person may harm another. Vet advice with your gastroenterologist.
Neglecting bone health and fall prevention, especially if you've used corticosteroids—osteoporosis is a silent complication. Ensure adequate vitamin D, calcium, and weight-bearing exercise.
Changing diet or supplements dramatically without guidance—well-intentioned changes can trigger flares or interact with medications.
Traveling unprepared (no medication backup, no knowledge of destination care)—preparation prevents crisis and allows continued disease management.
Overtraining or intense exercise during early remission—ease back into activity gradually. Intense exercise can trigger flares if done too soon.

specialists.role: Primary specialist for IBD diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment. Performs endoscopy, interprets imaging, prescribes and adjusts medications. Essential for ongoing care.
specialists.notes: Ideally a gastroenterologist with IBD subspecialty expertise (listed as 'IBD specialist'). First point of contact for new symptoms or flare concerns.

specialists.role: Helps identify trigger foods, optimize nutrition, educate on low-FODMAP or anti-inflammatory diet, and coordinate supplementation. Valuable complement to medical care.
specialists.notes: Many GI offices employ IBD dietitians; request referral. Not all dietitians understand IBD—specifically ask for IBD experience.

specialists.role: Manages comorbidities (hypertension, depression), routine preventive care, and coordinates specialist care. Important for holistic health.
specialists.notes: Can help identify early flare signs and make urgent referrals to gastroenterology.

specialists.role: Addresses anxiety, depression, and stress related to chronic illness. Offers cognitive behavioral therapy, mindfulness, or medication for mental health.
specialists.notes: Mental health comorbidity in IBD is common and treatable. Ask your GI doctor for referral or check if your insurance covers therapy.

specialists.type: IBD nurse educator or specialist nurse
specialists.role: Educates on disease management, medication injection (for biologics), and answers questions between doctor visits. May coordinate care and provide 24/7 phone support.
specialists.notes: Often employed by hospitals or infusion centers. Ask your gastroenterologist if one is available to you.

specialists.role: If extraintestinal manifestations (joint pain, axial spondylitis) are prominent, a rheumatologist can co-manage these conditions alongside your gastroenterologist.
specialists.notes: Referral is specific to autoimmune complications; most people with IBD do not need rheumatology.

patient_communities.name: Crohn's & Colitis Foundation
patient_communities.notes: Official US nonprofit with resources, local chapters, online support groups, and annual conference. Excellent for education and connecting with others.

patient_communities.name: r/IBD and r/CrohnsDisease and r/UlcerativeColitis
patient_communities.notes: Active online communities where people share experiences, ask questions, and discuss medications and diet. Moderated but unmedical advice; use for peer support and ideas to discuss with your doctor.

patient_communities.name: IBD Passport / GESA (Gastroenterological Society of Australia and New Zealand)
patient_communities.notes: Australasian resource with patient guides, support groups, and medical information.

patient_communities.name: CCUK (Crohn's and Colitis UK)
patient_communities.notes: UK-based charity offering education, online and in-person support, and advocacy. Equivalent to CCFA in North America.

patient_communities.name: Smart Patients or PatientsLikeMe
patient_communities.notes: Platforms where IBD patients track symptoms, share treatments, and connect anonymously. Useful for identifying patterns and discovering what others find helpful.

patient_communities.name: Facebook IBD support groups
patient_communities.url: Search 'IBD support' or 'Crohn's disease' on Facebook
patient_communities.notes: Many local and disease-specific groups exist. Quality varies; look for moderated groups with active engagement and supportive tone.

patient_communities.name: IBD Academy (webinars and online education)
patient_communities.notes: Free webinars on disease management, mental health, nutrition, and medication. Hosted by Crohn's & Colitis Foundation.

learning_resources.title: The Crohn's Disease and Ulcerative Colitis Fact Book
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive, patient-friendly overview of IBD causes, diagnosis, treatment, and lifestyle management. Useful for someone newly diagnosed or wanting a thorough foundational understanding.

learning_resources.title: Breaking the Vicious Cycle: Intestinal Health Through Diet
learning_resources.description: Describes the specific carbohydrate diet (SCD), a restrictive diet some IBD patients report helps manage symptoms. Includes meal plans. Anecdotal evidence; discuss with your dietitian before adopting.

learning_resources.title: The IBD Healing Plan & Recipe Book
learning_resources.description: Practical guide to identifying trigger foods and managing diet during flares and remission. Includes anti-inflammatory recipes. Bridges medical and practical dietary advice.

learning_resources.title: Crohn's & Colitis Foundation—Patient Education Hub
learning_resources.description: Free, evidence-based information on disease types, medications, surgery, nutrition, emotional health, and pregnancy. Regularly updated; highly credible.

learning_resources.title: IFFGD (International Foundation for Gastrointestinal Disorders) Educational Materials
learning_resources.description: Patient-friendly fact sheets on IBD, IBS, and GI health. High-quality, medical-reviewed content suitable for all education levels.

learning_resources.title: UpToDate (patient version)
learning_resources.description: Physician-reviewed summaries of IBD diagnosis and management written for patients. More detailed and technical than foundation resources but still accessible. Often available free through hospital patient portals.

learning_resources.title: The Crohn's Disease Cookbook: 150 Recipes by Registered Dietitian Nutritionists
learning_resources.description: Practical recipes designed to be gentle on the IBD digestive system while nutritionally balanced. Useful for remission meal planning.

learning_resources.title: The Psychology of Chronic Illness: Stress and Coping in Inflammatory Bowel Disease
learning_resources.description: Academic but accessible overview of how stress and psychology affect IBD. Reinforces the importance of mental health management alongside medical care.

learning_resources.title: Podcast: 'On Air with Ryan' by the Crohn's & Colitis Foundation
learning_resources.description: Interviews with IBD patients, doctors, and experts discussing real-world living with IBD, medications, mental health, and career/family challenges. Conversational and relatable.

learning_resources.title: Video: 'Understanding Your IBD Medications' by Crohn's & Colitis Foundation
learning_resources.description: Visual explainers of how different IBD medications (5-ASAs, biologics, immunosuppressants) work. Helpful for understanding your treatment.

learning_resources.title: Systematic Review: Maintenance Therapy in Ulcerative Colitis
learning_resources.author_or_source: Cochrane Collaboration or major GI journals
learning_resources.description: Evidence-based summary of which medications best maintain remission in ulcerative colitis. Useful if you want to understand the science behind your doctor's recommendations.

learning_resources.title: Patient Guide to Low-FODMAP Diet in IBD
learning_resources.author_or_source: Monash University or IBD-specific dietitian sites
learning_resources.description: Educational resource on the low-FODMAP diet, a technique for identifying trigger foods in IBS and some IBD cases. Includes food lists and practical tips.

sections.relevance_signal: **History of confirmed IBD** with ongoing IBS-like symptoms despite being in remission. Or: rectal bleeding, significant weight loss, nocturnal symptoms — these require exclusion of IBD.
